primary_diagnosis.name: Behcet disease (mild mucocutaneous subtype), acute oral aphthous ulcer flare threatening hydration

pathophysiology_summary: Behcet disease is a chronic, relapsing systemic vasculitis with immune dysregulation (neutrophil hyperactivity and T-cell mediated inflammation) whose obligatory and often earliest manifestation is recurrent oral aphthous ulceration. In the mild mucocutaneous subtype, disease is confined to oral (and sometimes genital/skin) surfaces without ocular, neurological or vascular involvement. This presentation is an acute flare: a rapid crop of painful buccal and soft-palate aphthae producing severe odynophagia. The immediate clinical danger is not the ulceration itself but its consequence — pain-driven inability to swallow leading to reduced oral intake and evolving volume depletion (here still mild: maintained blood pressure and mentation, adequate turgor, only orthostatic symptoms). Topical anaesthetic plus topical corticosteroid interrupt this loop by relieving pain and suppressing the local inflammatory flare, restoring the ability to hydrate and averting the need for intravenous rehydration or admission. Because only oral involvement is present, the diagnosis remains presumptive/incomplete for full Behcet criteria and requires longitudinal clinical confirmation; recurrent aphthous stomatitis is the principal benign mimic.

differential.evidence_basis: Most common cause of recurrent painful oral ulcers; must be actively considered before invoking a systemic cause.

differential.diagnosis: Dehydration secondary to reduced oral intake
differential.evidence_basis: The acute driver of urgency — inability to drink for 12 hours with orthostatic symptoms; the risk to manage now.
Can cause a painful acute oral eruption; distinguished by vesicles, gingival involvement and prodrome.
Oral enanthem possible; expect palm/sole lesions and often fever.
Recurrent oral aphthae are the obligatory feature; a systemic autoimmune cause must be held in mind though it cannot be confirmed at first contact.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, a competent clinician holds benign recurrent aphthous ulceration as most likely while treating the dehydration risk as the immediate priority — not anchoring on a rare systemic label.

differential.evidence_basis: Long history of recurrent oral aphthae now flaring severely; genital/ocular involvement screened negative (consistent with the mild mucocutaneous subtype).
Cannot be excluded remotely; overlaps clinically and shares topical management — longitudinal criteria distinguish it from Behcet.
No vesicles, no gingival swelling and no cold-sore history argue against HSV.
No palm/sole lesions and no fever.

differential.diagnosis: Dehydration secondary to reduced oral intake
differential.evidence_basis: Still the active management target — mild volume depletion (orthostatic dizziness, 12h no intake) with haemodynamics maintained.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The negatives (no genital/ocular lesions, no vesicles/cold sores, no fever/acral lesions) both narrow toward a recurrent aphthous / mild mucocutaneous picture and exclude infective mimics.

differential.evidence_basis: Best fit for severe recurrent oral aphthae; remains presumptive pending longitudinal confirmation of criteria.
Management-equivalent benign alternative that cannot be excluded via telehealth.

differential.diagnosis: Dehydration secondary to reduced oral intake
differential.evidence_basis: The complication being actively prevented; monitor because the patient lives alone.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot and need not settle Behcet vs recurrent aphthous stomatitis today — both are managed with the same topical therapy and safety-netting; the pass hinges on treating the flare, restoring hydration, choosing the correct urgent-but-not-emergency tier, and safety-netting for systemic evolution.

red_flags.description: Twelve hours of no oral intake with orthostatic lightheadedness — early volume depletion driving the urgency
red_flags.clinical_significance: This is the reason the presentation is urgent rather than routine: pain-driven inability to hydrate can progress to significant dehydration, especially in a person living alone.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to relieve pain and restore intake risks progression to significant dehydration requiring ED attendance and intravenous fluids.

red_flags.description: Complete inability to swallow even saliva / drooling
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals severe odynophagia with an at-risk airway and total loss of hydration route — an emergency-level escalation trigger.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Airway compromise or rapid severe dehydration if not escalated to ED.

red_flags.description: Established significant dehydration — no urine passed for more than 12 hours, progressive lightheadedness, or a fall on standing
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates decompensated volume depletion needing intravenous rehydration.

red_flags.description: New high fever or spreading/systemic features
red_flags.clinical_significance: Raises secondary infection or an alternative infective/systemic cause requiring reassessment.

red_flags.description: New eye redness, eye pain or visual disturbance
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ocular involvement in this disease spectrum is sight-threatening and marks evolution beyond the mild mucocutaneous subtype — needs urgent ophthalmology/specialist review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Permanent visual loss from untreated ocular inflammation.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals progression to systemic multi-site disease, changing the diagnosis and management toward systemic immunosuppression.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition and treatment of systemic disease.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dismissing the flare as trivial 'just mouth ulcers' and missing that the patient cannot hydrate and lives alone — under-triaging to self-care.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triage reflex on an autoimmune label
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Blanket-escalating a mild mucocutaneous flare to 000/ED despite maintained haemodynamics, clear mentation and adequate perfusion — an over-triage error.
Treating as herpes simplex stomatitis (antivirals) or hand-foot-and-mouth without noting the absent vesicles, cold-sore history, fever and acral lesions.
Failing to safety-net for new ocular symptoms, which in this disease spectrum are an emergency for vision.
Asserting a confirmed Behcet diagnosis remotely when only oral involvement is present and formal criteria and longitudinal follow-up are required.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct oropharyngeal examination (number, size, distribution of buccal and soft-palate aphthae)
Measured blood pressure and heart rate, and postural blood-pressure change
Mucous membrane moisture, capillary refill time and skin turgor on examination
Presence or absence of cervical lymphadenopathy
Objective confirmation of hydration status without a clinician assessment or bloods
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Manage as urgent same-day (T3): prescribe/advise topical anaesthetic plus topical corticosteroid to relieve pain and restore oral intake, give staged rehydration technique (numb then sip cool fluids), arrange a same-day script pathway, and safety-net explicitly for the dehydration and ocular/systemic escalation triggers. Do NOT default to 000/ED, and do NOT dismiss to pure self-care.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the safe move is to treat the flare and the hydration risk and to label the underlying cause presumptive (recurrent aphthous vs mild mucocutaneous Behcet), deferring definitive diagnosis to longitudinal clinical review rather than asserting or excluding Behcet remotely.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Recurrent oral ulceration is the cardinal feature; additional sites (genital, ocular, skin, vascular, neurological) define systemic involvement and severity.
Topical corticosteroids and topical anaesthetics are first-line for symptomatic aphthous ulceration; systemic therapy is reserved for severe or multi-site disease.
